title: Shared Aesthetic Experience, Community, and Meaningfulness
abstract: Aesthetic communities offer us opportunities for collective, communal, and value-disclosing shared aesthetic experiences. This paper develops an account of shared aesthetic experiences andprovides an answer to the question of their significance: when they occur within aesthetic communities, their distinctive phenomenology is a powerful resource for creating a sense that our lives are aesthetically meaningful.

title: What Would Beauty Have to Be In Order to Save the World?
abstract: Historically beauty has been seen to be one of the highest values. One thread in this tradition maintains that beauty has redemptive or salvific powers, that beauty has the power save, redeem, or transform, both individual lives and the world as a whole. This paper investigates what beauty would have to be in order to have certain salvific powers. The main negative conclusion is that a minimal condition is that the value of beauty be final rather than derivative. I argue against several contemporary views that make the value of beauty derivative. I then begin to sketch a view about beauty that both makes its value final and has the resources to explain why beauty has salvific powers.

title: The Resurrection of God?
abstract: In this essay I consider Stephen Mulhall’s project, in The Ascetic Ideal, to develop Nietzsche’s genealogy of the ascetic ideal. I explore the very idea of a genealogy and its potential for vindication or subversion; and I draw a distinction between what I call ‘primary’ vindication or subversion and ‘secondary’ vindication or subversion, before considering which of these is a feature either of Nietzsche’s own genealogy or of Mulhall’s development of it. I suggest that there is scope for Mulhall, if only by further developing Nietzsche’s genealogy, to remain faithful to Nietzsche’s own subversive intentions while nevertheless vindicating the ascetic ideal, both primarily and secondarily. In so far as what is at stake here is what Nietzsche calls ‘the death of God’, then another way to put this is to say that there is scope for Mulhall to accept the death of God while nevertheless heralding God’s subsequent resurrection.
si: Philosophy as Discourse: The Thought of Stephen Mulhall

title: Varieties of Aesthetic Response
abstract: I argue that there are at least three response-types that are capable of being responsive to the beautiful, and that these response-types are inequivalent. There can be aesthetic judgment without aesthetic appreciation, aesthetic appreciation without aesthetic judgment, and aesthetic appreciation without aesthetic understanding. On analogy with what persons call for, only rational judgment is required, even though the most excellent cases of responsiveness to beauty will encompass all three response-types.

title: On not acknowledging an animal’s wound: Reading Coetzee with Mulhall
abstract: Stephan Mulhall has a rare gift for bringing literary texts into dialogue with philosophical ones in ways that are mutually illuminating. In The Wounded Animal, he employs his talent on J.M. Coetzee’s The Lives of Animals and a wide variety of philosophical writing, including works by Cora Diamond and Stanley Cavell. Mulhall draws our attention to the wound borne by Elizabeth Costello, the protagonist of Coetzee’s story: she is profoundly alienated from her community, and she is tormented by her perceptions of others (including her own family members), as fundamentally kind and at the same time as morally depraved. Building on Mulhall’s description of Costello, I argue that she suffers from the failure of others to acknowledge her. In order to detail the toll that this failure exacts, I investigate the grammar of acknowledgment. I argue that mutual acknowledgment is essential both to social relationships and to our own understanding of ourselves. Costello’s community abandons her to her own tormenting perceptions, which no one else deigns to acknowledge. The result is one that our study of acknowledgment should lead us to expect: she is isolated, confused, and driven to the brink of madness.
si: Philosophy as Discourse: The Thought of Stephen Mulhall

title: What is Aesthetic Value?
abstract: Are beautiful things beautiful because they are valuable, or valuable because they are beautiful? Most theories of aesthetic value assume the former. I argue for the latter. This implies that no theory of aesthetic value can explain what makes aesthetically valuable things aesthetically valuable; instead, it can only tell us which value is the aesthetic one. I propose a theory that does.

title: Introduction: Beauty, Self, and Society
Introduction: Philosophy as Discourse: The Thought of Stephen Mulhall
si: Philosophy as Discourse: The Thought of Stephen Mulhall

title: Dwelling in the enigma: Heidegger, Being and human finitude
abstract: According to Heidegger, the notion of Being represents an enigma. On the one hand, we always experience entities in relation to their Being. On the other hand, since the Being of all these entities is not itself an entity, any attempt to articulate Being, and our experience of entities in relation to it, runs the risk of failing. It is enough to glance over the secondary literature to notice that philosophers have identified two ways in which Heidegger seems to overcome such a failure. According to what I call the non-propositional approach, Heidegger aims at conveying ineffable truths about Being and our experiencing entities in relation to it. According to what I call the eliminativist approach, Heidegger either does or should understand such a failure as a reductio ad absurdum of his own account of Being. After showing that both these interpretations are problematic, I carve out a third exegetical position by developing an analogy between Heidegger’s enigma of Being and the notion of a great riddle as defended by Cora Diamond and, in particular, Stephen Mulhall. Furthermore, I argue that, not only does my interpretation avoid the issues affecting both the non-propositional and the eliminativist approaches, but it also helps us to see how, according to Heidegger himself, the impossibility of overcoming the enigma of Being casts an important light on our finitude and, therewith, the existential posture with which we inhabit the world.
si: Philosophy as Discourse: The Thought of Stephen Mulhall

title: Disappointment with criteria: Cavell, Rhees, and skepticism
abstract: I discuss major themes in Stanley Cavell’s response to skepticism—the truth of skepticism, the openness of criteria to repudiation, and living one’s skepticism, and link these to the idea of conversation as explored in the writings of Rush Rhees.
si: Philosophy as Discourse: The Thought of Stephen Mulhall

title: A Kantian Account of Aesthetic Communitarianism
abstract: For aesthetic communitarians, social aesthetic goods are important—if not the most important—sources of aesthetic value. In this paper, I develop a Kantian account of aesthetic communitarianism that is distinct from contemporary accounts in focusing on the irreducibly communal dimension of judgments of beauty and the goods this affords. For Kant, a condition of making judgments of beauty is that one take oneself to belong to a universal community of taste, even if one is alone. Furthermore, the value of beauty lies in the shared feeling that this judgment constitutively involves.

title: Aesthetic Ranking: Tomatoes, Parker Points, and Pitch Forks
abstract: Despite the sustained social critique of the idea of an aesthetic canon, rankings of aesthetic items are ubiquitous and influential: film rankings, year-end lists, wine scores, album scores, social media about who or what is worse, better, and best. Why do we persist in doing this? Is it legitimate? A glance at some of the more influential ranking systems like Rotten Tomatoes, Pitchfork, and others reveals deep epistemic flaws—they tend to be exclusionary, distorted, or evaluatively opaque. How can we reconcile the ubiquity and apparent importance of aesthetic ranking with its manifest shortcomings? Here I argue that aesthetic rankings are “epistemically expropriative”. They are fundamentally aesthetic goods, and as such the norms of aesthetic valuing can override the epistemic rules that govern ranking systems. As aesthetic goods, these rankings promote the social exercise of our aesthetic capacities by promoting engagement, aesthetic conversation, comparative evaluation, self-expression, and aesthetic community. Aesthetic ranking may be epistemically flawed but it can be, even because of that, a powerful tool for the social practice of aesthetic valuing.

title: (In)Alienable: Acknowledgment and Its Limits
abstract: This paper considers Mulhall’s engagement with Ridley Scott’s Blade Runner, which he regards as “obsessed” with the question of what it is to be a human being. While Mulhall is surely right in identifying the film as so obsessed with this question, the answer that he develops out of his reading of the film is more problematic. Especially troubling is Mulhall’s enlistment of the concept of acknowledgment as a kind of linchpin for something’s securing human status. The appeal to acknowledgment in this context faces a kind of dilemma: either what is acknowledged already is human (and so acknowledgment is not what secures that being’s humanity) or it is not already human (in which case acknowledging it as human would be a pointless exercise). Mulhall’s appeal also raises questions concerning the status of those who are doing the acknowledging: what secures their human status such that they can acknowledge another being as human (or withhold that acknowledgment)? These questions invite renewed consideration of the skeptical problem of other minds, in particular the issue of whether the replacement of knowledge with acknowledgment concedes too much or too little to the skeptic.
si: Philosophy as Discourse: The Thought of Stephen Mulhall

title: “A Wedge-Shaped Core of Darkness”: On Self-Knowledge, Self-Opacity, and Soul-Pictures
abstract: This paper explores the idea that forms of self-opacity— vicissitudes of self-consciousness and disruptions of straightforward self-knowledge, registrations of dissonance and self-strangeness— need not be understood exclusively or necessarily as pathologies nor as brute psychological facts, but can be valued as part of a rich moral psychology and conception of selfhood. I consider a prominent contemporary account of self-knowledge that situates self-knowledge in the context of philosophical moral psychology and analyzes self-knowledge as an achievement of “rational, responsible self-directed agency.” While this model provides resources for understanding both self-knowledge and self-opacity, its broadly Kantian normative or axiological orientation means that forms of opacity tend to be framed as privations or pathologies of rational agency, in such a way as to leave us potentially cut off from the kinds of complexity, value, and significance that they might have for us. I then turn to some suggestive remarks by Bernard Williams, Lionel Trilling, and a passage from Virginia Woolf’s To the Lighthouse, that serve not only to indicate a way of thinking about the value of opacity, but suggest that attending to this value might call for imaginative and metaphorical forms of representation. This is to say that attending to this particular topic—the significance of self-opacity—involves attending to questions of philosophical and aesthetic style.
si: Philosophy as Discourse: The Thought of Stephen Mulhall

title: Walking and Falling: Mulhall and Cavell on Redemptive Steps in Wittgenstein’s Philosophical Investigations
abstract: In Philosophical Myths of the Fall, Stephen Mulhall argues that Wittgenstein’s Investigations harbors genuinely redemptive ambitions, but necessarily fails to achieve them. For him, Wittgenstein’s correct identification of the condition from which redemption is required—a structural flaw in human nature akin to the Christian idea of Original Sin—itself militates against the success of any redemptive efforts that, like Wittgenstein’s, do not rely on a divine source. Mulhall’s argument is a tour de force that seeks to read Wittgenstein through both Christian and Cavellian interpretive frames. However, there are deep tensions between these frames, and Cavell’s work provides good reasons to resist the view of Wittgenstein that Mulhall develops. In this essay, I draw on Cavell’s reading of Wittgenstein to contest Mulhall’s argument and to suggest that his Christian interpretive frame is ultimately more distorting than illuminating. However, while my focus is largely critical, my overall aim is positive; it is to use the examination of Mulhall’s argument to open more hopeful paths for thinking of the work of the Investigations as redemptive.
si: Philosophy as Discourse: The Thought of Stephen Mulhall

title: Mutual Vanities: On Being Understood by Art
abstract: This paper illuminates and defends an acceptable form of aesthetic vanity, or the experience of thinking an artwork is about you. To do so, I attend to what I call the individualizing power of artworks, which I argue is their capacity to facilitate the exploration and discovery of our individuality. Set against this backdrop, aesthetic vanity becomes an important aspect of a view of aesthetic flourishing according to which aesthetic engagement helps a life go well by serving as a vehicle of self-understanding.

title: Nothing Hidden
abstract: This paper discovers in the work of Jasper Johns, Edgar Allen Poe, and Michel Foucault a joint opposition to there being something hidden. Although someone may hide your present before the party, I mean to deny that there is anything hidden the superlative way logic was hidden in the Tractatus or the attenuated way painting was hidden for Michael Fried or the human for Stanley Cavell. In his mature writings, Wittgenstein sought a momentary peaceful healing of what we say and what we see. Now I can go on. Now it’s a Rabbit. But if nothing is hidden, the hope of philosophy might be to unbind what we see from what we say, to find a way to sink into the uregulated sensuality of things and the things-which-are-words: enjoying playing.
si: Philosophy as Discourse: The Thought of Stephen Mulhall

title: Bias Reduction as an Aesthetic Norm
abstract: Traditional aesthetic theories emphasize pleasure, while recent non-hedonistic approaches prioritize “getting it right” in aesthetic engagement. This paper critiques Dominic McIver Lopes and C. ThiNguyen’s theories by arguing that correctness is neither the necessary guiding norm nor the constitutive or right motivator. Instead, it proposes bias reduction—minimizing the improper influence of prior outlooks. This shift from correctness to minimizing distortion better captures aesthetic agency and allows for pluralism and radical disagreement.

title: Response to Lehigh Conference Papers
abstract: In this essay, I attempt to respond to the papers on my work contained in this volume of Philosophical Topics. My response attempts to draw out connections between those papers, as well as engaging with individual lines of criticism developed in each, so it is best thought of as one sustained critical evaluation of them all. It consequently touches on a number of inter-related issues, many of them deriving from Stanley Cavell’s work and its critical reception. These include the nature of religious belief and its relation to metaphysics; the nature of aesthetic judgement, its relation to artistic modernism and its specific application to film; the roles of knowledge and acknowledgement in articulating our relation with other human beings; and the ways in which Wittgenstein’s and Heidegger’s philosophical writings relate to one another.
si: Philosophy as Discourse: The Thought of Stephen Mulhall

title: Building a Better Aesthetic Hedonism
abstract: Aesthetic hedonism—the view that aesthetic value consists in pleasure—has faced serious objections in recent years, including James Shelley’s influential argument that it cannot account for the possibility of overvaluing an aesthetic object. Shouldn’t a hedonist aim for as much pleasure as possible? We propose an improved version of aesthetic hedonism that focuses on the whole of aesthetic life rather than isolated encounters with aesthetic objects. This shift reveals good reasons for the hedonist to care about correct evaluation, even when it leads to short-run losses in pleasure.

title: Almost Every Work of Art is a Failure
abstract: It is a constitutive aim of a work of art to express a point of view, but points of view are only had by persons. From this it follows that all works of art aspire to personhood. If a work of art ever succeeded in this aspiration, it would have a profound kind of value, but it cannot, and so its value, at least with respect to this aim, can never be other than that of a noble failure.
